title: Planted-Solution Pauli Hamiltonians as a Quantum Benchmarking Primitive
abstract: We introduce a construction of Pauli Hamiltonians with exactly known ground-state energies, intended as reference instances for ground-state energy estimation algorithms. The construction embeds a planted block-product state as the simultaneous ground state of a sum of frustration-free local clauses on overlapping supports, exposes the resulting model only as a polynomial-size linear combination of Pauli operators, and admits optional Clifford conjugation that preserves the spectrum. The framework subsumes classical planted constraint-satisfaction problems as a diagonal special case, providing a direct embedding channel through which classical hardness properties can be inherited. Open-source software, certification keys, and example instances are made publicly available.
summary: The paper introduces a rigorous method to construct Pauli Hamiltonians whose ground state energy is precisely known by design. By embedding a simple product state into a complex sum of local clauses, it creates challenging quantum benchmarks for ground-state solvers. This framework allows researchers to test the limits of quantum computation while maintaining full verifiability.
main_problem: The authors aim to develop reliable, verifiable benchmarks for quantum ground-state energy estimation algorithms that are designed to test the limits of quantum advantage.
main_result: They introduce a framework constructing Pauli Hamiltonians whose ground state and energy are known exactly by design, while presenting them in a complex, non-manifestly structured form.
method: The method constructs the Hamiltonian as a weighted sum of local clauses acting on overlapping supports, embedding an exactly known product state into the ground space.
model_or_system: The model is a quantum many-body system described by a Pauli Hamiltonian ($H$) constructed from planted block-product states. The structure allows for optional Clifford conjugation to scramble the operator support while preserving the spectrum.
key_observables: Ground-state energy ($E_0$), spectral gap ($\Delta_1$), and the size of the Pauli representation.
important_parameters: Clause density ($K/n$), system size ($n$), and the number of planted states ($m$).
main_assumptions: The resulting model can be represented as a polynomial-size linear combination of Pauli operators, and for multi-planted cases, blockwise orthogonality of planted states is assumed.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the construction pipeline (Partition $	o$ Clause Supports $	o$ Local Hamiltonians $	o$ Pauli Sum $	o$ Clifford Scramble). Figure 3 plots the intensive spectral gap vs. clause density for various system sizes.
paper_structure: The paper details the general construction of single-planted and multi-planted Hamiltonians, analyzes the exponential closing of the spectral gap ($\Delta_1 \sim c \cdot b^n$), and provides a worked example on $n=9$ qubits showing the embedding process.
why_interesting: This work provides a controlled theoretical playground to test quantum algorithms for finding ground states, offering benchmarks where the answer is known but the input structure is designed to be hard to exploit classically.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Construction pipeline for planted benchmark Hamiltonians. (a) Qubits are partitioned into disjoint blocks {Ai} with planted states |ψAi⟩defining a global product ground state |Ψ⋆⟩. (b) Random subsets Sk of blocks define overlapping clause supports Λk = S
FIG. 2. Fraction of instances with numerically degenerate ground state (∆1 < 10−10) as a function of K for different system sizes. As one would expect, the degeneracy fraction falls sharply with K.

FIG. 3. Intensive spectral gap ∆1/K as a function of clause density K/n for n = 6, 8, 10, 12. Bands indicate interquartile ranges over 50 instances. At fixed K/n, the intensive gap decreases approximately exponentially with n.

FIG. 4. Block-clause structure of the worked example. Nine qubits (numbered circles) are partitioned into five blocks A1– A5 (colored backgrounds) with non-contiguous membership. Four clause supports S1–S4 (shaded regions), each spanning three blocks, overlap to produce a fully connected constraint hypergraph. Planted block states are indicated above each cluster.
